title: The Nominative Case (Nominativ)
description: The nominative case is the base form of nouns and pronouns in German. It is used for the subject of a sentence — the person or thing performing the action — and after the verbs sein, werden, and bleiben.
whenToUse: For the subject of a sentence (the person or thing doing the action)
After the verbs sein (to be), werden (to become), and bleiben (to remain)
When asking questions with wer (who) or was (what)
In dictionary entries — nouns are always listed in the nominative

rules.title: The subject of a sentence is always in the nominative
rules.explanation: The subject is the person or thing performing the action of the verb. To find the subject, ask "Wer?" (who?) or "Was?" (what?) before the verb.

examples.correct: Der Hund schläft auf dem Sofa.
examples.explanation: "Der Hund" is the subject — it is performing the action of sleeping.
"Die Kinder" is the subject — they are performing the action of playing.

examples.correct: Das Buch liegt auf dem Tisch.
examples.explanation: "Das Buch" is the subject — it is performing the action of lying on the table.

rules.title: After sein, werden, and bleiben the nominative is used
rules.explanation: Unlike most verbs, sein (to be), werden (to become), and bleiben (to remain) link two nominative elements. The noun or adjective after these verbs refers back to the subject and stays in the nominative.

examples.explanation: After "ist" (sein), "ein guter Lehrer" stays in the nominative, not the accusative.

examples.incorrect: Sie wird eine berühmte Ärztin. (accusative would be wrong here)
examples.explanation: After "wird" (werden), the predicate noun remains in the nominative.
After "bleibt" (bleiben), the predicate noun stays in the nominative.

rules.title: Definite articles in the nominative
rules.explanation: Each grammatical gender has its own definite article in the nominative: der (masculine), die (feminine), das (neuter), and die (plural).

examples.explanation: "Der" is the masculine nominative article for "Mann".
"Die" is the feminine nominative article for "Frau".
"Das" is the neuter nominative article for "Kind".

rules.title: Indefinite articles in the nominative
rules.explanation: The indefinite articles in the nominative are: ein (masculine), eine (feminine), ein (neuter). There is no plural indefinite article; the noun stands alone or uses kein/keine.

examples.correct: Ein Vogel sitzt auf dem Baum.
examples.explanation: "Ein" is the masculine nominative indefinite article.
"Eine" is the feminine nominative indefinite article.

rules.title: Using wer and was to identify the nominative
rules.explanation: The question words "wer" (who) and "was" (what) are themselves in the nominative. The answer to a wer/was question is always the subject in the nominative.

examples.correct: Wer hat das gemacht? — Der Schüler hat das gemacht.
examples.explanation: "Wer" asks for the subject; the answer "der Schüler" is in the nominative.

examples.correct: Was liegt auf dem Tisch? — Das Buch liegt auf dem Tisch.
examples.explanation: "Was" asks for the subject; the answer "das Buch" is in the nominative.

commonMistakes.explanation: After "sein", use the nominative, not the accusative. The predicate noun matches the subject's case.
The subject must be in the nominative. "Den" is accusative and cannot be used for the subject.
"Problem" is singular, so no plural ending is needed. Article and noun must agree in number.
"Lehrerin" is feminine, so it requires the feminine article "die", not the masculine "der".
After "werden", no article change is needed — the noun stays in the nominative.

tips: Always ask "Wer oder was + verb?" to find the subject — the answer is in the nominative.
Remember: sein, werden, and bleiben never take an accusative object — both sides of these verbs are nominative.
The nominative is the form you find in dictionaries, so it is the best case to learn first.
In German, the subject does not always come first in the sentence, but it is still in the nominative regardless of position.
Learn the gender of each noun together with its nominative article (der/die/das) from the very beginning.
